coredata.dc:title: Porphyrin/carbon nanodot supramolecular complexes and their optical properties
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Villari, Valentina
Gaeta, Massimiliano
D’Urso, Alessandro
Micali, Norberto
coredata.dc:description: The functionalization of carbon dots with dyes by exploiting supramolecular interactions represents a huge resource in nanomaterials science. Desired properties, and especially the optical ones, can be obtained starting from the knowledge of surface-related interactions, which can be exploited to originate the complex. In this context, the present work explores the photophysical properties of supramolecular complexes, spontaneously formed, in the water medium, by the interaction of the anionic meso-tetrakis(4-sulfonatophenyl) porphyrin (TPPS4) with three different fluorescent carbon nanodots (CNDs). The results of UV–vis, steady-state and time-resolved fluorescence experiments show that the formation of porphyrin/carbon dot adducts is favoured if nanodot surface charge is high enough to ensure electrostatic attraction of the porphyrin, and the resulting emission properties depend on the surface precursor as well. In such adducts, both species undergo fluorescence quenching through non-radiative mechanisms such as electron transfer, making these nanomaterials economic candidates for applications in energy conversion or sensor field.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon Nanodots
Supramolecular complexes
Porphyrin
Fluorescence
Spectroscopy

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–vis spectrum of TPPS4 in the absence and in the presence of R,R-CND (plot a) and NCND (plot b) at different concentration values. Porphyrin concentration is fixed at 3 μM. The spectrum of CNDs alone is reported for comparison.

Fluorescence spectra (λex =350 nm) in the emission region of R,R-CNDs (data are corrected for the inner filter). The inset displays the fluorescence spectra normalized by CND concentration. Porphyrin concentration is fixed at 3 μM.

Plot of the ratio between emission intensity of CNDs alone, F0, and in the presence of TPPS4, F (left axis, filled circles), for R,R-CNDs (plot a) and for NCNDs (plot b) for λex = 350 nm and λem = 450 nm ([R,R-CND]=0.16 mg/ml; [NCND]=0.15 mg/ml). Hollow circles refer to the corresponding ratio between the nanodots’ average fluorescence lifetime (see right axis).

Fluorescence spectra in the emission region of TPPS4 (λex =500 nm) in the absence and in the presence of R,R-CNDs (plot a) and of NCNDs (plot b) at different concentrations. The spectra of CNDs in the absence of TPPS4 are also displayed (magenta curves).

Plot of the ratio between emission intensity (λex =500 nm, λem =655 nm) of TPPS4 alone, F0, and in the presence of R,R-CNDs (plot a) and NCNDs (plot b)nanodots, F (left axis, filled circles). The corresponding ratio between the lifetime of TPPS4 alone and in the presence of nanodots is also reported (right axis, hollow circles). The continuous line is the fit according to the Stern-Volmer equation 1a.
Absorbance spectrum of TPPS4 alone and in the presence of BCND (at fixed concentration of 0.1 mg/ml). The extinction spectrum of BCND alone at 0.1 mg/ml is reported for comparison.

Emission of BCNDs and BCND/TPPS4 solutions (λex =350 nm). A quenching is observed for all the emission bands, at wavelength values where porphyrin absorption is negligible (no inner filter effect). In the inset TPPS4 fluorescence in the absence and in the presence of BCNDs is reported (λex =350 nm).
Stern-Volmer plot for steady-state emission (filled circles) and fluorescence average lifetimes (hollow circles) of BCND (λex =350 nm; λem =450 nm).

head.ce:title: Porphyrin/carbon nanodot supramolecular complexes and their optical properties

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CNR-IPCF Istituto per i Processi Chimico-Fisici, Viale F. Stagno d'Alcontres 37, Messina 98158, Italy

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CNR-IPCF Istituto per i Processi Chimico-Fisici

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: CNR-IPCF Istituto per i Processi Chimico-Fisici, Viale F. Stagno d'Alcontres 37, 98158 Messina, Italy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Dipartimento di Scienze Chimiche, Università degli Studi di Catania, Viale A. Doria 6, Catania 95125, Italy

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Dipartimento di Scienze Chimiche, Università degli Studi di Catania

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Dipartimento di Scienze Chimiche, Università degli Studi di Catania, Viale A. Doria 6, 95125 Catania, Italy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The functionalization of carbon dots with dyes by exploiting supramolecular interactions represents a huge resource in nanomaterials science. Desired properties, and especially the optical ones, can be obtained starting from the knowledge of surface-related interactions, which can be exploited to originate the complex. In this context, the present work explores the photophysical properties of supramolecular complexes, spontaneously formed, in the water medium, by the interaction of the anionic meso-tetrakis(4-sulfonatophenyl) porphyrin (TPPS4) with three different fluorescent carbon nanodots (CNDs). The results of UV–vis, steady-state and time-resolved fluorescence experiments show that the formation of porphyrin/carbon dot adducts is favoured if nanodot surface charge is high enough to ensure electrostatic attraction of the porphyrin, and the resulting emission properties depend on the surface precursor as well. In such adducts, both species undergo fluorescence quenching through non-radiative mechanisms such as electron transfer, making these nanomaterials economic candidates for applications in energy conversion or sensor field.

ce:section.ce:para: The non-covalent functionalization via self-assembly still represents a powerful and promising approach for building supramolecular materials having novel and appealing optical features [1]. Yet, the supramolecular approach is even more challenging if the solvent employed to create functional devices is water. The spontaneous assembly opens the way to different mechanisms of interaction and, as a consequence, to the formation of different kinds of complexes. This is particularly useful for carbon-based nanomaterials, which exhibit energy conversion properties, and for carbon nanodots (CNDs) in particular, due to their peculiar emission properties and electron-transfer ability (as donors or acceptors) [2–8].

Indeed, the fluorescence of CNDs is the most appealing characteristic, although the mechanisms underlying their emission phenomenon have not been fully explained yet. This is mainly due to the huge number of investigated systems differing in the preparation method, size, purification, starting materials, surface oxidation and other parameters often difficult to control [9–15]. Although such issue is still controversial, it is unquestionable that the fluorescence properties of these emerging CNDs offer interesting perspectives for a broad range of new materials useful in sensing, bio-imaging, photodynamic therapy and energy-related applications [16–20].

Different systems are generally accepted under the meaning of carbon nanodots. Typically, carbon-based dots have a size lower than 10 nm and with a lattice or an amorphous internal structure mainly depending on their preparation method, i.e. top-down or bottom-up [16,21–24]. The latter approach expanded very quickly in the last years thanks to: (i) its easy realization, (ii) the achievement of good water solubility and (iii) the existence of surface groups available for binding with other molecules. Indeed, supramolecular interactions between CNDs and chromophores, polymers or biomolecules can be favoured by the existence of proper surface groups. Moreover, non-covalent binding with aromatic dyes discloses interesting aspects of CNDs photoluminescence, strictly related to sensing, energy conversion, photovoltaic applications and also photodynamic therapy [5,25–29].

In this regard, one of the most promising aspects of the CND-dye interaction is the investigation of the fluorescence quenching via photoinduced electron transfer (PET), a deactivation process involving a redox reaction between the excited state of the donor and the acceptor [30–32].

Among the dyes used in supramolecular chemistry, porphyrins are the most intriguing molecules owing to their unique spectroscopic features. Indeed, the interactions of porphyrin with other molecules are exploited to achieve supramolecular complexes with applications in a wide range of research fields [30,33–45].

The role of the electrostatic interactions and charge separation between negatively charged CNDs and positively charged porphyrins in supramolecular assemblies has been recently studied in methanol as solvent [30]. In addition, porphyrins have been suggested as chiroptical probe able to reveal the chirality of CNDs in the aqueous medium [46].

Here, our attention is devoted to the characterization of optical properties of porphyrin-CND supramolecular assemblies in the aqueous medium, where hydrophobic and π-stacking can be very relevant and, however, new routes of interactions are available. This type of characterization paves the way toward exciting opportunities for developing novel porphyrin-CND complexes with a wide range of applications. Prominent perspectives for dye-functionalised CNDs include their use as hybrid nanocomposites to enhance the power conversion efficiency in solar cells, to realize the conversion of solar energy into fuels or to employ their peculiar emission properties for the preparation of new light-emitting nanomaterials.

For exploring the formation and the interaction mechanisms of porphyrin-CND non-covalent complexes, the tetra-anionic meso-tetrakis(4-sulfonatophenyl)porphyrin (TPPS4) and three different CNDs with positive surface charge are chosen. The high molar extinction coefficient and fluorescence emission properties of TPPS4 allows for investigating ground- or excited-state interaction with CNDs. As a consequence of the interaction of dyes with CNDs, quenching of the fluorescence emission of the species occurs via static (formation of ground-state supramolecular complex) and/or dynamic (decrease of the excited-state lifetime under collision) mechanism [6,47,48]. The results of the present study highlight that TPPS4 is able to interact with CNDs according to supramolecular mechanisms generating ground-state complexes and causes non-radiative deactivation of the excited state reasonably via photoinduced electron transfer.

CNDs studied in this work were kindly gifted by Prof. Maurizio Prato’s group from University of Trieste. The preparation method and procedure, as well as the size and surface characterization of these particles, are reported by Prato et al. in references [5,24,46,49].

Three CNDs with different precursor mixtures are investigated here:
1)
R,R-CND: Arginine+ (R,R)-1,2-cyclohexanediamine [46,50];
2)
NCND: Arginine+ ethylenediamine [24];
3)
BCND: Arginine+ ethylenediamine+ boron-dipyrromethene [49]
CNDs were dispersed in bi-distilled pure water and prepared at different concentrations.

The CND/TPPS4 solutions, at different CND concentration, were prepared by adding a proper aliquot of TPPS4 (purchased from Sigma-Aldrich) stock solution (at 150 μM) to the CND solutions to obtain a final concentration of 3 μM, fixed for all the samples.
Another set of samples was prepared by fixing the CND concentration at the maximum value investigated and varying the amount of TPPS4 from 0.5 to 3 μM.
The pH was fixed at 7.0 for all the samples with a proper addition of HCl. All spectroscopic measurements were performed after an equilibration time of the solutions of 3 days.

Extinction spectra were collected by using the fibred Avaspec spectrometer (Avantes) with a resolution of 0.8 nm, equipped with fibred deuterium and halogen lamps (Avalight). Cuvettes of 10 mm of optical path were used.

The home-made set-up for measuring the fluorescence spectra exploits a Xenon lamp (150 W), two computer-controlled Oriel monochromators (focal 1/8 m and grating 1200 l/mm) for excitation and emission, and an analogic photomultiplier as detector. The slits were set in order to have a resolution of 4 nm. To minimize corrections for absorption and inner filter effects, the cuvettes with a pathlength of 3 mm were used.

Fluorescence data are discussed according to the Stern-Volmer approach for pure static and mixed static and dynamic quenching mechanism:
F 0
F
= 1 +
K
S V
Q
1a

F 0

=
K
S V

K D
+
K
S V
K D
Q
1b
static
and
dynamic
quenching

In the equations above F0 is the emission intensity of the fluorophore alone and F that in the presence of a different concentration of quencher, [Q]; KSV and KD are the static and dynamic quenching constant, respectively.
Depending on the excitation and emission wavelength, CND and porphyrin will be considered either fluorophore or quencher species.

Time-resolved fluorescence was acquired by exciting the samples with a polarized femtosecond Ti:Sa laser with duplication crystal, set at different wavelengths (350 and 500 nm). The beam was not focused on the sample to minimize photobleaching effects, whereas fluorescence was focused on the slit of the monocromator (focal 130 mm, holografic grating 1800 l/mm) and collected by a microchannel-plate photomultiplier (100 ps rising-time) operating in single-photon counting. EG&G electronic devices were used for pre-amplification, constant fraction discrimination, Time-to-Amplitude Conversion (TAC) and acquisition of time-resolved fluorescence curves [51,52]. These curves are fitted according to the following multiexponential law:
(2)
I
( t )
=
I
0
∑ i
A

exp
(
− t /
τ
i
)
where I(t) is total fluorescence, I0 is the intensity at time zero, and Ai and τi are the relative amplitude and lifetime of the i-th component, respectively, with
∑ i
A
i
= 1
. The average fluorescence lifetime is obtained as:
〈 τ 〉
=
∑ i
A
i
τ
i
2
∑ i
A
i
τ
i
.
The fitting is performed by means of the nonlinear least-squares iterative reconvolution procedures, obtaining an instrumental resolution of about few tens of picoseconds.
Cuvettes with path-length of 3 mm were used.

The measured fluorescence lifetimes are analysed in terms of the Stern-Volmer equation:
(3)
τ
0
τ
= 1 +
K
D
[ Q ]
with τ0 and τ the lifetime of the fluorophore alone and in the presence of different concentration of quencher, respectively.

The multiform features of supramolecular interactions are responsible for the peculiar optical properties observed in aqueous solutions of CNDs in the presence of porphyrin (TPPS4). Electrostatic, hydrophobic or π-π interactions favour the formation of adducts between R,R-CNDs and TPPS4, as well as between NCNDs and TPPS4, with charge transfer properties.

The occurrence of the interaction is evident from the significant hypochromism and the red-shift of the porphyrin Soret band ( Fig. 1a and b) for the solution with the highest content of CNDs at a certain fixed porphyrin amount. On decreasing the CND concentration, the spectral features of the solution containing TPPS4 alone tend to be recovered. Despite less pronounced, the same effect appears for the Q-bands. On the other hand, in the UV region of the spectrum, a slight red-shift and broadening of the CND bands have been observed. These spectroscopic features indicate electronic communication between the two species in the ground state and suggest the formation of a supramolecular adduct.

More information comes from steady-state and time-resolved fluorescence measurements, by inspecting the emission properties of CNDs and porphyrin under selective excitation at the absorption wavelength of the species. Although the fluorescence band of CNDs overlaps the absorption band of porphyrin, no evidence of energy transfer occurs. This clearly appears from the absence of enhanced porphyrin fluorescence under excitation at 350 nm (see Fig. S1).

The correction for the inner filter is not sufficient to eliminate the distortion of the profile of the CNDs emission, because of the overlap between the fluorescence band of CNDs and the strong Soret absorption band of TPPS4. For this reason in Fig. 2 the fluorescence spectra of R,R-CND/TPPS4 are reported above 450 nm, from which it is evident that the fluorescence emission of R,R-CNDs is quenched after the addition of TPPS4. Furthermore, at constant porphyrin amount and lower nanodots concentration, emission simply scales with R,R-CNDs concentration, or, in other words, quenching scales accordingly. This suggests that there is an excess of TPPS4 in the solution and that almost all nanodots are involved in the interaction with porphyrin. Analogous results are obtained for the NCND/TPPS4 solutions (Fig. S2).

The time-resolved fluorescence of CNDs is characterized by two lifetimes; both components depend on the emission wavelength (see Table S1), indicating the existence of different emitting states [14,53].

Under excitation at 350 nm and emission at 450 nm (where TPPS4 does not emit), the most significant difference between R,R-CNDs and NCNDs is their fluorescence quenching mechanism in the presence of porphyrin. Indeed, the steady-state fluorescence of R,R-CNDs is quenched according to a parabolic trend as a function of TPPS4 concentration, whereas NCNDs fluorescence follows a linear behaviour (see Figs. 3 and S3). In addition, the Stern-Volmer plot for the CNDs average fluorescence lifetime, < τ > , displays a linear dependence on TPPS4 concentration for R,R-CNDs and a constant behaviour for NCNDs (see also Table S2). These findings indicate the occurrence of a mixed static and dynamic quenching mechanism for R,R-CNDs and only a pure static one for NCNDs [54]. Therefore, both CNDs species are able to form static complexes with TPPS4, but on the surface of R,R-CNDs some states remain still available for collisional or distance-dependent quenching.

By the linear fit (performed using Eq. (3)) of R,R-CNDs average fluorescence lifetime (Fig. 3a, dashed line), the dynamic quenching constant, KD, of about 0.095 μM-1 is obtained. The static contribution of the quenching mechanism for the R,R-CND/TPPS4 complex is extracted from the modified Stern-Volmer plot as about 0.013 μM-1 (equation 1b and Fig. S4). On the other hand, the fit with equation 1a of the unmodified Stern-Volmer plot gives a static quenching constant of 0.11 μM-1 for the NCND/TPPS4 complex (Fig. 3b).

Therefore, upon binding with porphyrin, the CNDs state involved is statically quenched (for both kinds of CNDs), but R,R-CNDs seem to possess other available states which are quenched under collisional events or interactions with TPPS4 less close to the surface [54]. The latter occurrence could be due to the larger steric hindrance of the bulkier cyclohexanediamine precursor than ethylenediamine, which prevents some porphyrins from a closest approach to the emitting surface sites.

In order to collect the porphyrin emission (in the region above 620 nm), the samples are excited at 500 nm (the stronger Q band of TPPS4) where absorption values below 0.1 are guaranteed. Indeed, in such an emission region fluorescence of CNDs is low and the spectra are dominated by the emission of porphyrin, which decreases with the increasing of CND concentration, as displayed in Fig. 4a and b.

After subtracting the small fluorescence contribution of CNDs at the proper concentration, the ratio between the fluorescence intensity of free TPPS4 and that in the presence of CNDs linearly depends on the nanodot concentration ( Fig. 5), so obeying the Stern-Volmer equation 1a. In this case F0 and F are the emission intensity of TPPS4 alone and in the presence of different concentration of nanodots, respectively.
The value of KSV, obtained by the fit of F0/F data in Fig. 5, is about 8.8 ml/mg for porphyrin in the presence of R,R-CNDs and about 1.4 ml/mg for porphyrin in the presence of NCNDs.

In order to gain more details on such a quenching phenomenon, the results from time-resolved fluorescence measurements are reported. The time-resolved fluorescence is still bi-exponential at higher CND contents, but the shorter component disappears at lower CND contents (Table S3); in addition, the longer component remains the same as free TPPS4 at any CND concentration. Therefore, the shorter lifetime can be reasonably ascribed to CNDs and the longer one to porphyrins. Moreover, the invariance of porphyrin lifetime suggests a static fluorescence quenching mechanism, due to the formation of a non-fluorescent (or de-activated on few picosecond timescale) supramolecular complex. The quenching efficiency of porphyrin emission is higher for R,R-CNDs than for NCNDs.

Such a static mechanism of quenching indicates that porphyrins are able to interact and come into contact with the surface of both kind of CNDs. The comparison with the results obtained above, by exciting nanodot emission, could support the hinted hypothesis about the distance that different porphyrins at the surface of R,R-CNDs have from the emitting nanodot sites.

An analogous efficient non-radiative deactivation was observed for the NCNDs carbon nanodot conjugated covalently with a porphyrin [5], as well as for a CND/porphyrin supramolecular system in methanol [30], and was attributed to an electron transfer phenomenon. Therefore, in analogy to those systems, the above described findings can be rationalized by considering that an electron donor/acceptor system is formed, the absorbed energy being spent for the charge separation process.

Because both CNDs possess a positive surface charge [5,50], it is reasonable to expect that electrostatic interactions represent a relevant factor in the complex formation. In order to check this hypothesis, the BCNDs nanodots, which have a lower surface charge (the zeta potential is about +2 mV) with respect to NCNDs [5], are also considered.
The absorption and emission spectrum of BCNDs displays the characteristic bands of both BODIPY and of nanodots ( Fig. 6).

Hypochromism of the porphyrin Soret band is observed in the presence of BCNDs, which is accompanied by a small fluorescence quenching of TPPS4 at 650 nm ( Fig. 7, inset). No red-shift of the TPPS4 emission and a small quenching of the BCNDs emission at 450 nm are observed, along with fluorescence lifetimes quenching (Table S4). The Stern-Volmer plot reported in Fig. 8 indicates the same dependence for the steady-state and time-resolved results, so pointing to a pure dynamic quenching mechanism (collisional).

Because the quenching of the fluorescence intensity at 520 nm follows exactly the same trend as that at 450 nm (Fig. S5) (and no changes are observed in lifetimes, Table S4), it appears plausible that the band at 520 nm experiences the intensity decrease originated at 450 nm. No further quenching mechanisms need to be involved.

Therefore, all our data suggest that the spectroscopic changes of BCNDs are due to the different solvent experienced by the nanodots because of the presence of porphyrin and that no ground-state complex is formed. The lower surface charge, in fact, weakens the electrostatic interaction, which appears to play a key role in the formation of a complex.

In summary, it is deduced that: (i) the electrostatic contribution is mainly responsible for the formation of the supramolecular complex, in which porphyrins macrocycle can interact with the carbon dot surface via π-π or charge-transfer interactions; (ii) the fluorescence quenching of TPPS4 in the complex can occur via electron transfer; (iii) the fluorescence quenching of carbon dots seems to depend on the availability of the emitting sites to porphyrin molecules at the particle surface; iv) the quenching mechanism of the nanodots is undoubtedly related to their surface structure: in particular, when the steric hindrance prevents some porphyrins from the closest approach to the emitting surface sites, the distance-dependent quenching can act along with the static mechanism.

There are clear findings indicating that the carbon nanodots studied, independently from the precursor mixture used, are able to form supramolecular complexes with an anionic porphyrin if the surface charge is high enough to ensure electrostatic attraction. The interaction mechanism between nanodots and porphyrins involves their ground states through significant intermolecular electronic interactions and causes changes in the emission properties through non-radiative paths.

The non-radiative path can be triggered by the formation of a donor/acceptor system through photo-induced electron transfer, as observed recently for the covalent counterpart and in supramolecular assemblies in organic solvent.

Once formed, the supramolecular complexes display quenching mechanisms that depend on the carbon dot precursor mixture. In particular, the experimental results suggest that a bulkier precursor at the nanodot surface can hinder the proximity of some porphyrins to the emitting sites, so preventing the extinguishing of their emission.

Undoubtedly, the supramolecular complexes based on the interaction between porphyrins and carbon nanodots seem to be the pathway to realize promising functional materials. Therefore, the knowledge of the nanodot/porphyrin surface-related interactions helps to design and realize adducts with the desired photophysical properties for many potential applications, from energy conversion devices to “turn off-on” sensors. Nevertheless, the further comprehension of spectroscopic phenomena of such assemblies may also result in the formation of new supramolecular materials with intriguing photophysical aspects for photothermal and photodynamic therapy.

In addition, the more economic and easy-to-prepare strategy behind these new materials can also represent a promising tool for better performance and new opportunities. In this context, the low toxicity and good water solubility of carbon dots suggest, as future perspective, the chance to substitute the dye with biologically relevant molecules like catecholamines, involved in various biological phenomena and degenerative diseases.
The experimental activity was supported by MUR (Ministero dell’Università e della Ricerca, Italy) with project PRIN_2017YJMPZN.

Valentina Villari: Conceptualization, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft; Massimiliano Gaeta: Investigation, Writing – review & editing; Alessandro D’Urso: Investigation, Writing – review & editing; Norberto Micali: Methodology, Investigation, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank Prof. M. Prato (University of Trieste – Department of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Sciences), Dr. F. Arcudi and Dr. L. Ðordević (University of Trieste – Department of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Sciences; current affiliation: Northwest University – Department of Chemistry) for providing the carbon nanodots used in this study.

ce:note.ce:simple-para: The zeta potential measured for R,R-CNDs in aqueous solution is about +5.5 mV.
